text: Lazarus' sickness is not unto death but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified by it.
He calls the disciples to go back into Judaea again despite the danger awaiting him there.
He who walks in the day does not stumble because he sees the light of this world; but he who walks in the night stumbles for he has no light — Lazarus sleeps, and Jesus goes to wake him.
He tells them plainly that Lazarus is dead, and is glad he was not there so that they might believe — let us go to him.
He assures Martha that her brother shall rise again.
He asks the mourners where they have laid Lazarus.
He commands that the stone be removed, reminds Martha that belief sees God's glory, thanks the Father who always hears him, calls Lazarus forth from the tomb, and orders that he be loosed and let go.
He defends Mary's anointing — she has kept this for the day of his burying; the poor are always with you, but he will not always be present.
The hour has come that the Son of man should be glorified.

He who loves his life loses it, while he who hates his life in this world keeps it eternal; whoever serves Jesus must follow him — though his soul is troubled, he will not ask the Father to save him from this hour, but prays that the Father's name be glorified.
The voice from heaven came for the people's sake — now is the judgment of this world, and the prince of this world shall be cast out.
Yet a little while is the light with you — walk while you have the light lest darkness overtake you, and believe in the light that you may become children of light.

He who believes in Jesus believes in the One who sent him, and seeing him is seeing the Father; he came as light into the world so that believers should not abide in darkness, and came not to judge but to save.
He has not spoken from himself, for the Father who sent him gave commandment what to say, and that commandment is life everlasting.
What he is doing Peter does not now understand but will know later — and unless Peter is washed, he has no part with Jesus.
He who is washed needs only to wash his feet and is clean every whit — and the disciples are clean, but not all of them.
He asks if they understand what he has done; they rightly call him Master and Lord, for so he is.

He has chosen the Twelve, yet scripture must be fulfilled that one who eats bread with him will lift his heel against him; he tells them beforehand so they may believe — and one of them shall betray him.
He identifies the betrayer as the one to whom he gives the dipped sop, then tells Judas, what you do, do quickly.
Now is the Son of man glorified and God glorified in him; little children, yet a little while he is with them, and where he goes they cannot come.
Where he goes Peter cannot follow now, but shall follow afterwards.
He questions Peter's claim to lay down his life and foretells that the cock shall not crow until Peter has denied him three times.
Where he is going they know, and the way they know.
If they had known him, they would have known the Father also — and from now on they know him and have seen him.
He who believes in Jesus shall do the works he does, and greater works, because he goes to the Father.
If you love me, keep my commandments.

He will not leave them comfortless but will come to them; because he lives they shall live also, and shall know that he is in the Father, they in him, and he in them — and the one who keeps his commandments loves him and shall be loved by the Father.
If a man loves him he will keep his words, and the Father will love him, and they will come and make their abode with him; the word they hear is not his own but the Father's.
He has told them beforehand that they might believe; the prince of this world is coming and has nothing in him, but the world must know that he loves the Father — arise, let us go.
They are already clean through the word he has spoken to them.
If the world hates them they should know it hated him first; because they are not of the world but chosen out of it, the world hates them.

The world will persecute them for his name's sake because it does not know the Father; if he had not come and done unprecedented works among them they would have no sin, but now they have seen and hated both him and the Father — fulfilling the scripture, they hated me without a cause.
And they also shall bear witness, because they have been with him from the beginning.
He has spoken these things so that they should not be offended.

Persecutors will act against them because they have known neither the Father nor him; he tells them now so they will remember when the time comes — now he goes to the One who sent him, and sorrow has filled their hearts.
He has many things yet to say to them, but they cannot bear them now.

All things the Father has are his, therefore the Spirit will take of his and show it to them — a little while and they shall not see him, and again a little while and they shall see him, because he goes to the Father.

They shall weep and lament while the world rejoices, but their sorrow shall be turned to joy — like a woman in travail who forgets her anguish for joy that a child is born — and their joy no one will take from them.
He has spoken in proverbs, but the time comes when he will show them plainly of the Father; in that day they shall ask in his name.
He asks if they truly believe now, for the hour comes when they shall be scattered every man to his own and leave him alone — yet he is not alone, for the Father is with him.
The disciples have known that all things given to him are from the Father, for he gave them the Father's words, which they received and believed that the Father sent him.
All things that are his are the Father's, and all that are the Father's are his — and he is glorified in his disciples.

While he was with them he kept them in the Father's name, and none was lost but the son of perdition; now he comes to the Father so they might have his joy fulfilled in themselves, though the world has hated them because they are not of it, even as he is not.
His disciples are not of the world, even as he is not of the world.
O righteous Father, the world has not known you, but he has known you, and these have known that you sent him.
Going forth to those who came to arrest him, he asks, Whom do you seek?
He commands Peter to put up his sword — shall he not drink the cup the Father has given him?
Before the high priest he answers that he spoke openly to the world, taught in synagogue and temple, and said nothing in secret — let those who heard him be asked, for they know what he said.
If he has spoken evil, let the officer bear witness of the evil; but if he has spoken well, why is he struck?
He asks Pilate whether he says this of himself, or whether others have told him concerning Jesus.
His kingdom is not of this world; if it were, his servants would fight to keep him from being delivered to the Jews — but his kingdom is not from here.
Pilate would have no power against him at all unless it were given from above; therefore the one who delivered him to Pilate has the greater sin.
Appearing again to the disciples eight days later with Thomas present, he greets them, Peace be unto you.
From the shore he asks the fishermen if they have any meat, and tells them to cast the net on the right side of the ship, where they will find a catch.
He instructs them to bring of the fish which they have just caught.
He invites the disciples, Come and dine.
